title: Work by an expanding gas: area under the P–V curve
scenario: One mole of an ideal gas at $T = 300$ K expands isothermally from $V_1 = 0.025$ m³ to $V_2 = 0.075$ m³. How much work does it do — and why is the answer a logarithm, not simply pressure times volume change? Watch the work accumulate as the area under the P–V curve.

assumptions.claim: Ideal gas: $PV = nRT$ holds exactly (no intermolecular forces, point particles).
Isothermal, reversible, quasi-static expansion — the temperature is held constant by a reservoir.
Work is the area under the P–V curve, $W = \int P\,dV$, with no friction or dissipation.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two memorized cases)
steps.latex: W = P\,\Delta V \quad(\text{constant pressure}) \qquad W = nRT\ln\!\frac{V_2}{V_1}\quad(\text{constant temperature})
steps.prose: At constant pressure, work is pressure times volume change — a rectangle. For an isothermal expansion you are handed a logarithm to memorize, with no account of where it comes from. The algebra course can use both but cannot say why one is a rectangle and the other a logarithm.

rectangle_work.label: If pressure stayed at $P_1$: $W = P_1\,\Delta V$ (the rectangle)

steps.label: Work is the area under the pressure–volume curve
steps.prose: Work done by the gas is the integral of pressure over volume — the area under the $P$–$V$ curve. At constant pressure the area is a rectangle ($P\,\Delta V$); when the pressure varies, it is the area under a curve.

steps.label: Use the ideal-gas law at constant temperature
steps.prose: For an isotherm, $PV = nRT$ makes $P$ inversely proportional to $V$ — a hyperbola. The area under $1/V$ is a logarithm, so the memorized isothermal work is exactly the area under the curve. Its slope at any volume is $P(V)$.

steps.label: Why the pressure case is the special one
steps.prose: When pressure does not vary, the integrand is constant and the integral collapses to the rectangle $P\,\Delta V$ — the algebra formula is the quadrature, the area already evaluated. The expanding-gas work is less than this rectangle because the pressure falls as the gas expands.

proof.heading: The work is the area under the P–V curve — and the memorized isothermal result falls out.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{dW}{dV} = P$; check $W = \int_{V_1}^{V} P\,dV'$; recover $W = nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$; collapse the constant-pressure case to $P\,\Delta V$

checks.claim: The work's slope is the pressure: $\tfrac{dW}{dV} = P$ — the area's rate of growth is the curve's height.
The accumulated work is the area: $W(V) = \int_{V_1}^{V} P\,dV'$.
The memorized isothermal result $W = nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$ is exactly the area at $V_2$.
At constant pressure the integral collapses to $W = P\,\Delta V$ — the area is a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: The work done by an expanding gas is just pressure times the change in volume, $W = P\,\Delta V$.
misconception.refuted_by: That is only true at *constant* pressure (a rectangle). In an isothermal expansion the pressure *falls* as the gas expands — $P = nRT/V$ — so the work is the area under a curved hyperbola, $nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$, which is less than $P_1\,\Delta V$. Drag the cursor: the shaded area under the curve, not a rectangle, is the work.

practice.asks: the work done by the expanding gas
practice.prompt: One mole of gas at $T = 300$ K expands isothermally from $V_1 = 0.025$ m³ to $V_2 = 0.075$ m³. How much work does it do?

choices.misconception: Used the rectangle $P_1\,\Delta V$ — pressure times volume change at the *initial* pressure. But the pressure *falls* as the gas expands, so the true work is the smaller area under the curve, $nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$.
Dropped the $\ln(V_2/V_1)$ factor — wrote $W = nRT$ instead of $W = nRT\ln(V_2/V_1)$. That logarithm *is* the area under the $1/V$ curve.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (two memorized cases)
algebra_steps.latex: W = P\,\Delta V \quad(\text{constant pressure}) \qquad W = nRT\ln\!\frac{V_2}{V_1}\quad(\text{constant temperature})
algebra_steps.prose: At constant pressure, work is pressure times volume change — a rectangle. For an isothermal expansion you are handed a logarithm to memorize, with no account of where it comes from. The algebra course can use both but cannot say why one is a rectangle and the other a logarithm.
